title: How to pick the right EV electricity tariff
description: How to choose an EV electricity tariff in the UK: compare off-peak rates, weigh up higher day rates, and cut home charging to around 2p per mile.
blurb: A practical method for choosing an EV tariff: off-peak rates, day-rate trade-offs, and the sums that show your real saving.
lede: Charging an electric car on a standard variable tariff is like filling a petrol car at a motorway services every single time: you pay the worst available price for no good reason. A dedicated EV tariff with a cheap overnight window can cut your charging cost by 70 to 80 per cent, which for a typical driver is several hundred pounds a year. But the cheapest headline rate is not automatically the best deal, because most EV tariffs charge a little more during the day, and the right choice depends on how many miles you drive, how much electricity the rest of your house uses, and whether your charger and car can work with a smart tariff. This guide gives you the method, with real numbers, so you can pick the right tariff for your own situation rather than someone else's.
short_answer: Pick an EV tariff by comparing the whole-bill cost, not just the overnight rate. At the July to September 2026 price cap of 26.11p per kWh, a typical 8,000-mile driver pays around £600 a year to charge at home; on an EV tariff with off-peak rates of 5.5p to 9p per kWh, the same charging costs £125 to £210. The catch is a daytime rate that is often a few pence above the cap, so the tariff wins when your overnight charging (plus any appliances you shift) outweighs the extra cost of daytime use. For most EV drivers doing more than about 4,000 miles a year, it does, comfortably.

sections.h2: The four types of EV tariff, and who each one suits
sections.html:
Suppliers package EV electricity in four broad ways. Knowing which type you are looking at matters more than the brand name on it, because the trade-offs are the same across suppliers. We cover the mechanics in more detail in our guide to how EV electricity tariffs work, but here is the short version.

  • Single-rate (standard) tariffs. One price all day, currently capped at 26.11p per kWh on a standard variable deal. Fine if you barely drive, charge mostly away from home, or cannot get a smart meter. For everyone else, this is the expensive default.
  • Two-rate EV tariffs. A cheap window, typically 4 to 6 hours overnight at roughly 7 to 9p per kWh, and a day rate that is usually at or slightly above the price cap level. Simple, predictable, and the right answer for most EV drivers. You set your charger to run inside the window and that is the whole job.
  • Intelligent (managed) tariffs. The supplier controls when your car charges, slotting it into the cheapest and greenest periods, sometimes outside the advertised window. In exchange you get the lowest rates on the market, around 5.5p per kWh on the best-known example in mid 2026. These need a compatible car or charger, and you have to be comfortable handing over scheduling.
  • Dynamic (half-hourly) tariffs. The price changes every 30 minutes with the wholesale market, occasionally dropping to near zero or below. Cheapest of all for people who can shift load aggressively, but prices can also spike on calm winter evenings. See our guide to Agile and tracker-style tariffs before choosing this route.

If you are new to all this, start by assuming a two-rate EV tariff is your answer, then check whether an intelligent tariff is available for your car and charger combination, since that is usually where the extra saving lives.

sections.h2: What the right tariff is worth in real money
sections.html:
The gap between tariff types is not marginal. Here is what a year of home charging costs for a typical EV doing 8,000 miles at 3.5 miles per kWh, which works out at roughly 2,300 kWh of charging including a small allowance for losses.

How you chargeRate per kWhCost per mileAnnual cost (8,000 miles)
Standard variable tariff26.11p7.5p£600
EV tariff, upper off-peak rate9p2.6p£207
EV tariff, typical off-peak rate7p2.0p£161
Intelligent tariff, lowest mid-2026 rate5.5p1.6p£127
Public rapid charging only79p22.6p£1,817

Standard rate 26.11p per kWh is the Ofgem price cap for July to September 2026. Off-peak examples of 5.5p to 9p and the 79p public rapid average are mid-2026 figures; check current tariff rates before switching.

Moving from the standard rate to a typical 7p off-peak rate saves about £440 a year on charging alone. For context, the same 8,000 miles in a 45 mpg petrol car costs around £1,285 in fuel at 159p a litre, so an EV on the right tariff runs at roughly an eighth of the petrol cost. Our EV versus petrol running cost comparison works through that side of the sum in full.

The table also shows why tariff choice matters more than almost any other EV decision: the spread between the best home rate and relying on public rapids is nearly £1,700 a year for the same mileage.

sections.h2: Step one: work out your own charging demand
sections.html:
Before comparing tariffs, you need three numbers of your own. Generic advice fails here because a 4,000-mile-a-year driver and a 20,000-mile company car driver should make different choices.

  • Annual mileage. Take it from your MOT history or insurance renewal rather than guessing. People routinely overestimate.
  • Your car's real efficiency. Most EVs achieve 3 to 4 miles per kWh in mixed UK driving, less in winter. Your car's trip computer will show a long-term average.
  • Annual charging kWh. Divide mileage by efficiency, then add about 10 per cent for charging losses. 8,000 miles at 3.5 miles per kWh is about 2,290 kWh before losses, call it 2,500 kWh from the wall.

Our step-by-step charging cost calculator guide walks through this with worked examples if you want to be more precise. Then multiply your charging kWh by each candidate tariff's off-peak rate to see what the car costs on each deal. While you are at it, note your household's total annual electricity use from your bill, because you will need it for the next step.

One more honest question: how much of your charging actually happens at home? If you have no driveway, or you do high motorway mileage with lots of rapid charging, the home rate matters proportionally less, and the guide to charging without a driveway is the better starting point.

sections.h2: The catch: higher day rates, and how to do the blended sum
sections.html:
EV tariffs are not charity. The cheap overnight window is usually paid for by a daytime rate a penny or three above the standard cap, and sometimes a slightly different standing charge. Whether the tariff wins overall depends on the balance between your cheap-window use and your daytime use. Here is the comparison method, using a realistic example.

Take a household using 2,900 kWh a year for the house plus 2,300 kWh for the car, 5,200 kWh in total.

  • On the standard variable tariff: 5,200 kWh at 26.11p is about £1,358 a year for units.
  • On a two-rate EV tariff with a 7p off-peak rate and a 28p day rate: the car costs 2,300 kWh at 7p, £161. Suppose you also shift 20 per cent of household use into the cheap window by running the dishwasher and washing machine overnight: 580 kWh at 7p is £41, and the remaining 2,320 kWh at 28p is £650. Total: about £852.

That is a saving of roughly £500 a year, even though every daytime unit costs nearly 2p more. The higher day rate cost the household about £44 extra; the cheap charging saved over £540. The sum only turns against you at very low mileage, where a few hundred kWh of cheap charging cannot offset thousands of kWh of slightly dearer daytime use.

Three rules of thumb fall out of this method:

  • Below roughly 3,000 to 4,000 miles a year of home charging, check the sum carefully; the standard tariff or a dual-rate deal like Economy 7 may be close enough not to bother.
  • The more household load you can shift into the window, the better an EV tariff looks. Dishwashers, washing machines and immersion heaters on timers all count.
  • If you have a home battery or storage heaters, the cheap window becomes even more valuable, because you can fill them at 7p and use the energy during the day.

sections.h2: Practical requirements before you can switch
sections.html:
A few boxes need ticking before any EV tariff will take you, and a couple more before an intelligent tariff will.

  • A working smart meter. Every EV tariff needs one, sending half-hourly readings so the supplier can bill the windows correctly. If yours is in dumb mode or you do not have one, your supplier should fit one free; sort this first, since lead times vary.
  • A way to schedule charging. A dedicated home charger with scheduling, or a car that can schedule its own charging from a three-pin granny lead, is enough for a two-rate tariff. Our guide to home EV charging costs covers the charger question and what installation adds.
  • Compatibility, for intelligent tariffs. The supplier needs to control the session, either through an API link to your car or through a supported charger. Check the supplier's compatibility list against your exact car model and charger before signing up, because this is the most common reason an application bounces.
  • Window length versus your charging need. A 7 kW charger delivers about 42 kWh in a 6-hour window, roughly 150 miles of range. If you regularly need more than the window can deliver, an intelligent tariff that charges outside the fixed window at the cheap rate, or a longer-window tariff, suits you better than the absolute lowest rate.

Timing the sessions themselves is its own small craft, covered in our guide to the best time to charge an electric car, but the short version is: let the schedule do the work, plug in every evening, and stop thinking about it.

For most EV drivers who charge at home, a dedicated EV tariff is the single biggest running-cost lever available, worth £400 to £500 a year against the standard cap for an average-mileage driver and far more at high mileage. Pick it with a method, not a headline rate: work out your annual charging kWh, multiply by each candidate's off-peak rate, then add your household's daytime use at each tariff's day rate and compare whole-bill totals. Choose a simple two-rate tariff if you want predictability, an intelligent tariff if your car or charger is supported and you want the lowest cost per mile, and a dynamic tariff only if you enjoy managing it. Recheck the sum once a year or whenever rates change, because the EV tariff market moves faster than the rest of the energy market, and last year's best answer often is not this year's.

faq.q: Do I need a smart meter to get an EV tariff?
faq.a: Yes. EV tariffs bill different rates at different times of day, which needs half-hourly readings from a working smart meter. If you do not have one, or yours has lost its connection, your supplier should fit or fix one free of charge, and it is worth arranging before you apply for the tariff.

faq.q: Will an EV tariff make my daytime electricity more expensive?
faq.a: Usually a little, yes. Most EV tariffs set the day rate at or slightly above the standard price cap level to pay for the cheap overnight window. For a typical driver the cheap charging saves far more than the dearer daytime units cost, but at very low mileage you should do the whole-bill comparison before switching.

faq.q: Can I get an EV tariff if I only charge from a three-pin plug?
faq.a: Often, yes. Two-rate tariffs generally only require that you charge during the window, which a car-side charging schedule can handle on a granny lead. Intelligent tariffs are stricter: they need a compatible car or a supported smart charger so the supplier can manage the session. A three-pin lead also only adds around 13 to 14 kWh in a six-hour window, which limits how much cheap charging you can take on.

faq.q: Is a dynamic half-hourly tariff better than a fixed off-peak EV tariff?
faq.a: Only if you can shift most of your usage flexibly and tolerate price spikes. Dynamic tariffs can beat fixed EV rates across a year for engaged users, but prices rise sharply on still winter evenings. A fixed two-rate or intelligent EV tariff gives most drivers nearly all the saving with none of the monitoring.

faq.q: Can I use the cheap overnight window for the rest of the house too?
faq.a: Yes, and you should. Dishwashers, washing machines, tumble dryers, immersion heaters and home batteries can all run or charge in the window at the same off-peak rate as the car. Shifting even 20 per cent of household use overnight adds a worthwhile extra saving on top of the charging itself.
